title: American Seabed Protection Act
plain_summary: The American Seabed Protection Act stops the US government from allowing companies to mine deep underwater areas that belong to America. The only exception is when scientists need to do research. The bill also requires a study to find out how underwater mining could hurt sea animals and damage the ocean environment. This law is meant to protect the deep parts of the ocean from being harmed by mining activities.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR E86-87)

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the American Seabed Protection Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Prohibition of Certain Mining Activities on Deep Seabed and Outer Continental Shelf
sections.text:
Congress has found several important facts about ocean health and mining. The United Nations says the world's oceans are in great danger from changes in the atmosphere, taking resources from the ocean, and pollution. The United Nations created a goal in two thousand fifteen to protect and carefully use oceans, seas, and ocean resources. Scientists and policy experts around the world agree that deep seabed mining is very dangerous to ocean life. This mining causes direct loss of unique and important species. It creates large clouds of dirt that harm ocean areas far from mining sites. It makes noise pollution that stresses ocean animals. It will likely pollute fish that people eat for food. It will likely hurt the ocean's ability to store carbon and fight climate change.

The United Nations Convention on Biological Diversity says member countries should make sure that before allowing deep seabed mining, they must study the impacts on ocean life. They must understand the risks to ocean systems. They must create strong rules to make sure mining does not harm ocean life. A two thousand twenty-two United Nations report says financing deep seabed mining does not follow sustainable ocean finance principles. There is not enough scientific information about the deep sea to fully understand the risks of deep seabed mining.

No license, permit, or other permission can be given for exploring or commercially taking minerals from the deep seabed, even though other laws might allow it. No license, permit, or other permission can be given for exploring, developing, or producing hardrock minerals on the Outer Continental Shelf (the underwater land that belongs to the United States), even though other laws might allow it. These rules do not apply to scientific research activities.

Within ninety days after this law is passed, the Secretary must try to make an agreement with the National Academies of Science, Engineering, and Medicine to do a complete study of how mining affects the environment on the deep seabed and Outer Continental Shelf. The Secretary must give Congress a report about what the study finds.

The study must include a description of ocean systems on the deep seabed and Outer Continental Shelf, in the water above these areas, and on underwater mountains and hot water vents. It must assess how mining could affect living things and their homes in these areas, including how big the impact would be, how long it would last, and whether these areas could recover. It must look at how mining could affect the deep sea's ability to store greenhouse gases. It must study how mining could affect people who use the ocean, including fishing businesses, people who fish for fun, people who farm fish, and companies that build underwater structures. It must look at how mining could affect indigenous peoples and cultures connected to ocean life.

The study must assess how dirt clouds from disturbing the seabed and discharge from mining ships could affect ocean species and food chains. It must estimate greenhouse gas pollution from seabed mining, including pollution that could happen when mining changes the biology, rocks, or chemistry of the seabed or water above it. It must assess whether there are good alternatives to using minerals from the deep seabed and Outer Continental Shelf, including better recycling and reuse of minerals, developing substitute materials with less environmental harm, and reducing environmental damage from land-based mining.

The law defines several terms. Appropriate Congressional committees means the House committees on Foreign Affairs and Natural Resources, and the Senate committees on Commerce Science and Transportation, Energy and Natural Resources, and Foreign Relations. Commercial recovery, deep seabed, development, exploration, Outer Continental Shelf, and production have the meanings given in existing laws about seabed resources and continental shelf lands. Secretary means the Secretary of Commerce, working through the head of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.
